blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلْفَجْرِ (١)
By the Dawn, (1)
وَلَيَالٍ عَشْرٍ (٢)
by the ten nights, (2)
وَٱلشَّفْعِ وَٱلْوَتْرِ (٣)
by the Even and the Odd, (3)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَسْرِ (٤)
by the night when it departs! (4)
هَلْ فِى ذَٰلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِّذِى حِجْرٍ (٥)
Is there an oath in that for one possessing intellect? (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah Al-Fajr

This Surah, like many others revealed in Makkah, consists of short, soul-shaking, and resonant verses, accompanied by numerous warnings.

In the first part of this Surah, we encounter several oaths that are unprecedented in their kind, and these oaths serve as an introduction to threatening tyrants with Divine punishment.

In another part of this Surah, reference is made to some of the rebellious nations of the past, such as the people of ‘Ad, Thamud, and Pharaoh, and Allah’s severe vengeance upon them, so that other powers may take account of themselves.

In the third part, in connection with the previous sections, there is a brief mention of the trial and testing of man, criticizing his shortcomings in performing righteous deeds.

In the final part of this Surah, it addresses the issue of the Resurrection and the fate of criminals and disbelievers, as well as the great reward for the believers who possess "peaceful souls" (Nufus Mutma'innah).

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, we read in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites it during the Ten Nights (the first ten nights of Dhu al-Hijjah), Allah will forgive his sins; and whoever recites it during other days, it shall be a light for him on the Day of Resurrection." [^1] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Recite Surah Al-Fajr in your obligatory and recommended prayers, for it is the Surah of Husayn ibn Ali عليه السلام. Whoever recites it will be with Husayn ibn Ali عليه السلام on the Day of Resurrection in his degree in Paradise." [^2]

[^1] and [^2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 481.

The introduction of this Surah as the "Surah of Husayn ibn Ali" عليه السلام may be because the clearest manifestation of the "peaceful soul" (al-nafs al-mutma'innah) addressed in the final verses of this Surah is Husayn ibn Ali عليه السلام, as mentioned in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding these very verses.

Or it may be because one of the interpretations of "the Ten Nights" (Layalin 'Ashr) is the first ten nights of Muharram, which have a special connection to Husayn ibn Ali عليه السلام.

In any case, all these rewards and virtues belong to those who make its recitation a prelude to their own reformation and self-building.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

وَ الْفَجْرِ (1) وَ لَيالٍ عَشْرٍ (2) وَ الشَّفْعِ وَ الْوَتْرِ (3) وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا يَسْرِ (4)

هَلْ فِي ذلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِذِي حِجْرٍ (5)

Commentary

By the Dawn I Swear!

At the beginning of this Surah, five awakening oaths are mentioned:

First, He says: "By the dawn and the splitting of the black veil of night" (وَ الْفَجْرِ).

"And by the ten nights" (وَ لَيالٍ عَشْرٍ). "Fajr" (dawn) originally means a wide splitting, and since the light of morning splits the darkness of night, it is referred to as "Fajr." We know that there are two types of dawn: "false" (kadhib) and "true" (sadiq).

The false dawn is that long whiteness that appears in the sky, likened to a fox's tail, where its narrow point is toward the horizon and its conical base is in the middle of the sky.

The true dawn spreads across the horizon from the very beginning; it has a special purity, luminosity, and transparency, covering the eastern horizon like a stream of clear water, and then spreading across the entire sky.

The true dawn announces the end of the night and the beginning of the day; at this time, those fasting must begin their abstinence, and the time for the Morning Prayer (Salat al-Fajr) begins.

Some have interpreted "Fajr" in this verse in its absolute sense, meaning the break of dawn, which is certainly one of the signs of Allah's greatness. It is a turning point in the lives of humans and all earthly creatures, marking the beginning of the sovereignty of light and the end of darkness, the start of movement and activity for living beings, and the end of sleep and silence. Because of this life, Allah has sworn by it.

However, some have interpreted it as the "dawn of the beginning of Muharram," which is the start of the new year.

Others interpret it as the "dawn of the Day of Sacrifice" (Eid al-Adha), when the important rituals of Hajj take place, and which is connected to the ten nights. Finally, some refer it to the dawns of the holy month of Ramadan or the "dawn of Friday morning."

However, the verse has a broad meaning that encompasses all of these, although some instances are clearer and more significant than others.

Some have considered the meaning of the verse even broader, saying that "Fajr" refers to any light that shines in the heart of darkness.

Therefore, the shining of Islam and the pure light of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله in the darkness of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) is one of the manifestations of "Fajr." Similarly, the shining of the dawn of the uprising of al-Mahdi عليه السلام when the world is submerged in the darkness of tyranny and oppression is considered another manifestation (as pointed out in some narrations). [^1] The uprising of Husayn on Ashura in that bloody plain of Karbala, splitting the dark veils of Umayyad tyranny and revealing the true face of those demon-like figures, was another manifestation.

Likewise, all true revolutions throughout history and today that take place against disbelief, ignorance, and oppression are included.

Even the first sparks of awakening that appear in the dark hearts of sinners, inviting them to repentance, are "Fajr."

But of course, this is an expansion of the verse's concept, while the literal meaning of the verse is the same—

[^1]: This meaning is narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) (Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 457, Hadith 1).

—Fajr meaning the rising of the dawn.

As for "the ten nights" (Layalin 'Ashr), the famous interpretation is the first ten nights of Dhu al-Hijjah, which witness the greatest and most soul-stirring socio-political worship gatherings of the world's Muslims.

This meaning is narrated in a Hadith from Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [^1] Some have also interpreted it as the last ten nights of the holy month of Ramadan, which contain the Nights of Power (Laylat al-Qadr).

And some as the first ten nights of Muharram.

Combining these three interpretations is also entirely possible.

In some narrations referring to the inner meanings (batn) of the Quran, "Fajr" is interpreted as the existence of Imam al-Mahdi عليه السلام, "the ten nights" as the ten Imams before him, and "the even" (al-shaf') which comes in the next verse as Ali and Fatimah al-Zahra عليهما السلام.

In any case, the oath by these ten nights, regardless of the interpretation, is evidence of their extraordinary importance, as oaths are always taken by significant matters. [^2] A combination of meanings is also possible.

Then, continuing the oaths, He adds: "And by the even and the odd" (وَ الشَّفْعِ وَ الْوَتْرِ).

[^1]: "Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi," Vol. 12, p. 74.

[^2]: Although "Layalin 'Ashr" is mentioned here in the indefinite form (nakirah), given that the indefinite form here is to express greatness, it takes on a specific meaning referring to those particular nights mentioned above.

What is meant by "the even" (al-shaf') and "the odd" (al-watr) in this verse?

Commentators have mentioned numerous opinions and possibilities: some up to twenty opinions [^1] and some have gone beyond that, citing up to thirty-six opinions! [^2] The most important of them are the following:

  1. It refers to even and odd numbers. According to this interpretation, Allah has sworn by all numbers, which consist of even and odd—numbers upon which all calculations and systems revolve and which encompass the entire universe. It is as if He said: "I swear by order and calculation." Indeed, the most important matter in the universe is this issue of order, calculation, and number, which also forms the basic foundation of human life.

  2. "The even" refers to the creatures, as all of them have a pair or a counterpart, and "the odd" refers to Allah, who has no likeness, peer, or equal. Furthermore, all contingent beings are composed of "essence" and "existence," which in philosophy is referred to as a "compositional pair" (zawj tarkibi); only the infinite existence without essence is the pure Being of Allah. (This interpretation is pointed out in some narrations from the Infallibles). [^3]

  3. It refers to all the creatures of the world, which from one perspective are even and from another are odd.

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 31, p. 164.

[^2]: Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan cited this from some commentators (Vol. 20, p. 406)—in the commentary "Ruh al-Ma'ani," this is cited from the book "al-Tahrir wa al-Tahbir" (Vol. 30, p. 120).

[^3]: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri narrated this from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) (Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 485).

  1. It refers to the prayers, some of which are even in the number of units (rak'ahs) and some are odd (this meaning is also narrated in a Hadith from the Infallibles عليه السلام), or it refers to the Shaf' and Watr prayers performed at the end of the Night Prayer (Nafilah al-Layl).

  2. "The even" refers to the Day of Tarwiyah (the 8th of Dhu al-Hijjah when pilgrims prepare to move to Arafat) and "the odd" refers to the Day of Arafah when the pilgrims of the House of Allah are in Arafat; or "the even" refers to the Day of Sacrifice (the 10th of Dhu al-Hijjah) and "the odd" refers to the Day of Arafah (this interpretation also appears in narrations from the Infallibles). [^2]

The main point is that if the definite article (al-) in these two words is for generality, all these meanings can be combined within it, as each of these interpretations and others are manifestations of "the even" and "the odd," and mentioning any one specifically does not mean exclusivity, but rather an application to a clear instance.

However, if the definite article is for specification (al-ahd), it refers to a specific even and odd. Here, in accordance with the previous oaths, two meanings are most suitable: first, that it refers to the Day of Sacrifice and the Day of Arafah, which perfectly suit the ten nights of the beginning of Dhu al-Hijjah, and the most important parts of the Hajj rituals are performed on them; or that it refers to the prayers, in accordance with the oath by the dawn, which is the time of the early morning and the time of intimate supplication to the Lord, especially since both interpretations are found in narrations from the Infallibles.

Finally, in the last oath, He says: "And by the night when it—

[^1] and [^2]: "Same source."

—departs toward the morning and the light of day" (وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا يَسْرِ). [^1] What an interesting expression, attributing "walking" (yasri) to the night itself! And that too, walking at night (for "yasri" is from the root "sara," which according to Raghib in al-Mufradat means walking at night).

It is as if the night is a living being with senses and movement, stepping through its own darkness and moving toward a bright morning.

Yes, an oath by that mentioned darkness that is heading toward light—a moving darkness, not a stagnant or fixed one. Darkness is terrifying when it becomes fixed, but if there is movement in it toward the light, it is highly valuable.

Some have also said that the darkness of night is in motion across the surface of the globe, and fundamentally, what is important, useful, and life-giving about the night is this very night that is in motion—that is, it constantly and alternately replaces the day. If the night were fixed on one half of the globe, that half would die, as would the other half which would be under the constant glare of the sun!

Regarding what is meant by "the night" here—is it all nights or a specific night?

Again, there is discussion among commentators. If the definite article (al-) means generality, it includes all nights, which are themselves a sign among the signs of Allah and an important phenomenon of creation.

If the definite article is for specification, it refers to a specific night, and in accordance with the previous oaths, it refers to the night of the Day of Sacrifice (the night of Eid al-Adha), when pilgrims move from Arafat to Muzdalifah (al-Mash'ar al-Haram) and, after spending the night in that holy valley—

[^1]: "Yasri" was originally "yasri," the present tense verb from the root "sara"; then its "ya" was omitted for brevity and harmony with the preceding verses.

—they head toward the land of Mina at sunrise. (This interpretation also appears in narrations from the Infallibles). [^1] Those who have seen the scene of that night in Arafat and Mash'ar up close know how thousands upon thousands of people are moving from every corner; one feels as if the night itself is moving there with its entire being. It is true that the pilgrims are moving, but this general movement is so vast that it is as if all of time and space are in motion, and this is only felt when a person is in that land on the night of Eid and sees the meaning of "وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا يَسْرِ" with their own eyes.

In any case, whatever the meaning of night (general or specific), it is one of the signs of Divine greatness and one of the highly important subjects of the universe. Night moderates the air temperature, grants tranquility to all creatures, and provides a peaceful atmosphere for intimate prayer to Allah. As for the night of the Day of Sacrifice, which is called "Laylat al-Jam'" (the Night of Gathering), it is also one of the most wondrous nights of the year in that holy valley of al-Mash'ar al-Haram.

In any case, the connection between these five oaths (the oath by the dawn, the ten nights, the even, the odd, and the night when it moves) is clear if we consider them all as referring to the days of Dhu al-Hijjah and the great rituals of Hajj.

Otherwise, they refer to a collection of important events in the realms of creation and legislation, which are signs of Allah's greatness and wondrous phenomena in the universe.

After mentioning these meaningful and awakening oaths, He adds: "Is there not in that—

[^1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 571.

—an oath [sufficient] for one of sense?" (هَلْ فِي ذلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِذِي حِجْرٍ).

"Hijr" here means intellect (aql), and its original meaning is "prevention" or "restriction." For example, it is said the judge "hajar" (restricted) so-and-so, meaning he prevented him from disposing of his property. Or a room is called "hujrah" because it is a protected place restricted from others entering it. The lap is also called "hijr" because of protection and prevention. Since the "intellect" also prevents a person from incorrect actions, it is referred to as "hijr," just as the word "aql" itself means "prevention" or "restraint"; hence the rope used to tie a camel's knee to prevent it from moving is called "iqal."

Regarding what the "subject of the oath" (muqsam bihi—the thing for which these oaths were taken) is, there are two possibilities: First, that the sentence "إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ" (Indeed, your Lord is in observation) is the response to these oaths.

Second, that the response to the oath is omitted, and the upcoming verses that speak of the punishment of the transgressors are evidence for it. The meaning would be: "I swear by what has been mentioned that We shall surely punish the disbelievers and the transgressors." [^1] In this way, both the oath and its subject become clear.

[^1]: The implied meaning is: "We shall surely punish the disbelievers and the transgressors."

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ (٦)
Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with [the people of] ‘Ad, (6)
إِرَمَ ذَاتِ ٱلْعِمَادِ (٧)
[and] Iram, [the city] of the pillars, (7)
ٱلَّتِى لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُهَا فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (٨)
the like of which was not created among cities, (8)
وَثَمُودَ ٱلَّذِينَ جَابُوا۟ ٱلصَّخْرَ بِٱلْوَادِ (٩)
and [the people of] Thamud, who hollowed out the rocks in the valley, (9)
وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِى ٱلْأَوْتَادِ (١٠)
and Pharaoh, the impaler (10)
ٱلَّذِينَ طَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (١١)
—those who rebelled [against Allah] in their cities (11)
فَأَكْثَرُوا۟ فِيهَا ٱلْفَسَادَ (١٢)
and caused much corruption in them, (12)
فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ (١٣)
so your Lord poured on them lashes of punishment. (13)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِٱلْمِرْصَادِ (١٤)
Indeed your Lord is in ambush. (14)

Commentary

Your Lord is in Ambush for the Oppressors

Following the previous verses, which contained meaningful oaths regarding the punishment of transgressors, these verses point to several powerful nations of the past—each of whom possessed immense strength but rode the steed of pride and chose the path of rebellion and disbelief. It clarifies their painful fate so that the polytheists of Makkah and other nations, who were perhaps much weaker than them, may take account of themselves and awaken from the sleep of negligence.

First, He says: "Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with 'Ad?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعادٍ).

The meaning of "seeing" (رؤیت) here is "knowledge and awareness." However, because the stories of these nations were so famous and well-known that it was as if the people of later times saw them with their own eyes, the term "seeing" is used.

Of course, the addressee in this verse is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but it serves as a warning for everyone.

"‘Ad" was the people of the great Prophet of God, Hud عليه السلام. Some historians believe that the name ‘Ad applies to two tribes: a tribe that lived in the very distant past, whom the Quran refers to as "‘Ad the first" (عاداً الْأُولىٰ) (An-Najm: 50).

They likely lived in prehistoric times.

The second tribe existed during human history, possibly around 700 BC, and is famous by the name "‘Ad." They lived in the land of "Ahqaf" or "Yemen."

They had tall statures and powerful bodies, and for this reason, they were considered elite warriors.

Furthermore, they were advanced in terms of outward civilization; they had prosperous cities, fertile and green lands, magnificent palaces, and vibrant gardens.

Some say "‘Ad" is the name of the ancestor of this tribe, and a tribe was usually called by the name of its ancestor.

Then He adds: "[With] Iram, the city of lofty pillars" (إِرَمَ ذاتِ الْعِمادِ). There is a discussion among commentators whether "Iram" is the name of a "person," a "tribe," or a place and "city."

Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf narrates from some that "‘Ad" was the son of "‘Aws," who was the son of "Iram," who was the son of "Shem," the son of "Noah" عليه السلام. Since the name of the tribe's ancestor was applied to the tribe itself, the tribe of ‘Ad was also called "Iram."

Some also believe that "Iram" is the same as "‘Ad the first," and ‘Ad is the second tribe, while others believe "Iram" is the name of their city and land. [1] However, consistent with the following verse, it is more appropriate that "Iram" be the name of their peerless city.

[1]: Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 747. Al-Qurtubi also mentions the same in his commentary, as do other commentaries.

"‘Imad" (عماد) means "pillar," and its plural is "‘Amad" (عمد).

According to the first interpretation, it refers to the powerful stature and pillar-like bodies of the people of ‘Ad. According to the second interpretation, it refers to the magnificent buildings, lofty palaces, and the massive pillars used in those palaces. In both cases, it is an indication of the power and strength of the people of ‘Ad. [1] However, the second interpretation (meaning the massive pillars of their palaces) is more suitable.

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "The like of which had not been created in [all] the lands" (الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُها فِي الْبِلادِ). This expression shows that "Iram" refers to the city, not the tribe or clan.

Perhaps for this reason, some great commentators have accepted this interpretation, and we have also preferred it. [2] Some commentators have narrated a detailed story about the discovery of the beautiful and magnificent city of "Iram" in the deserts of the Arabian Peninsula and the wilderness of Aden, speaking of its grand architecture and extraordinary, peerless ornaments. However, the said story resembles a legend or a dream more than a reality! Nevertheless, there is no doubt that the people of ‘Ad and their cities were among the strongest tribes and most advanced lands, and as the Quran indicates, the like of them was not found in the lands.

There are also many stories circulating in oral tradition and history about the "Paradise of Shaddad," who was the son of ‘Ad, to the extent that the "Paradise of Shaddad" and the "Gardens of Iram" have become proverbial. However, these stories are more like "myths"—myths that have a root in reality but have had many embellishments added around them.

[1]: According to the first interpretation, the femininity of "Dhat" (ذات) is because of the word "tribe" (Ta'ifah), which is grammatically feminine. [2]: "Iram" is diptote (ghayr munsarif), and therefore it is marked with fatha in the state of jarr.

Then He moves to the second group of transgressors from the ancient nations and says: "And [with] Thamud, who hollowed out the rocks in the valley" and built houses and palaces from them (وَ ثَمُودَ الَّذِينَ جابُوا الصَّخْرَ بِالْوادِ). The people of "Thamud" are among the oldest nations, and their Prophet was Salih عليه السلام. They lived in a land called "Wadi al-Qura" between "Madinah" and "Sham" (Syria). They had an advanced civilization, a comfortable life, and were owners of magnificent and sophisticated buildings.

Some have said: Thamud was the name of the father of the tribe, by which they were named. [1] "Jabu" (جابوا) is originally derived from "Jawbah" (جوبة), meaning low land, and then it came to mean cutting and carving any piece of land. The response to speech is called "Jawab" (answer) because it is as if it cuts through the air and reaches the listener's ear from the speaker's mouth (or because it cuts off the question and ends it).

In any case, the meaning here is carving pieces of the mountains and creating secure houses, just as we read in verse 82 of Surah Al-Hijr regarding the same people of Thamud:

[1]: "Thamud" (ثمود) etymologically comes from the root "Thamad" (ثمد), meaning a small amount of water that has no source. "Mathmud" is said of someone from whom much wealth is demanded until his wealth decreases. Some also consider this word to be non-Arabic (Raghib's Mufradat).

وَ كانُوا يَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً آمِنِينَ: "And they used to carve houses from the mountains, [feeling] secure." A similar meaning appears in verse 149 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, where the expression buyutan farihin (houses with skill/exultation) is used, which indicates that they engaged in luxury and indulgence in these houses.

Some have said the people of Thamud were the first nation to undertake the carving of stones from mountains and the creation of sturdy houses within the heart of the mountains.

"Wad" (واد), which was originally "Wadi" (وادى), means a riverbed or a path for floodwaters, and sometimes it also means "valley," because floodwaters pass through the valleys next to mountains.

Here, the second meaning—valleys and foothills—is appropriate, because according to other verses of the Quran that speak of this nation (as mentioned above), the people of Thamud built their houses on the slopes of mountains by carving stones and creating secure dwellings within them. [1] It is mentioned in a Hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, during the Battle of Tabuk on his way to northern Arabia, reached the valley of Thamud. While riding his horse, he said: "Hasten, for you are in a cursed and condemned land." [2] Undoubtedly, the people of Thamud also had an advanced civilization and prosperous cities in their era, but here again, we encounter figures and numbers that seem exaggerated or legendary, such as what a group of commentators have written: that they had built one thousand seven hundred cities, all of which were made of stone!

[1]: The "Ba" (بـ) in "جابُوا الصَّخْرَ بِالْوادِ" apparently carries the meaning of location (zarfiyyah). [2]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 425.

Then He turns to the third nation, saying: "And [with] Pharaoh, owner of the stakes" (وَ فِرْعَوْنَ ذِي الْأَوْتادِ).

This is an indication: Have you not seen what Allah did with the powerful, oppressive, and tyrannical people of Pharaoh? "Awtad" (أوتاد) is the plural of "Watad" (وتد), meaning stake or peg.

There are various interpretations as to why Pharaoh was called "Owner of the Stakes." First, he possessed a vast army, many of whom lived in tents, and the military tents erected for them were secured with stakes.

Another is that Pharaoh's most common form of torture for those who incurred his wrath was to "four-stake" them; he would fasten their hands and feet to the ground with stakes, or nail them to the ground, or lay them on a piece of wood and nail or tie their hands and feet to it, leaving them in that state until they died! This interpretation is narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. [1] As recorded in history, when his wife "Asiya" believed in "Moses" عليه السلام, he tortured and killed her in this same manner.

Another interpretation is that "Owner of the Stakes" is fundamentally a metaphor for power and the stability of government.

Of course, these three interpretations are not mutually exclusive and may all be encompassed in the meaning of the verse.

[1]: ‘Ilal al-Shara’i‘, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 571, Hadith 6.

Then, in a summary, He refers to the actions of these three nations and adds:

"[All] those who transgressed within the lands" (الَّذِينَ طَغَوْا فِي الْبِلادِ).

"And increased therein much corruption" (فَأَكْثَرُوا فِيهَا الْفَسادَ). Corruption (Fasad), which includes every kind of oppression, tyranny, transgression, lust, and debauchery, was in fact one of the effects of their rebellion. Every rebellious nation eventually sinks into all-encompassing corruption.

Then, in a short and meaningful sentence, He refers to the painful punishment of all these rebellious nations, adding: "So your Lord poured upon them a scourge of punishment" (فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذابٍ). "Sawt" (سوط) means "whip" or "scourge," and originally it means mixing one thing with another. Later, it was applied to a whip woven from various strands of leather and the like. Some consider it a metaphor for punishment—a punishment that mixes with the flesh and blood of a person and causes him severe distress.

In the words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, regarding trial, we read:

"By Him who sent him with the truth, you shall be confused with confusion, and sifted with sifting, and stirred as with a ladle in a pot [until the bottom becomes the top and the top becomes the bottom]." [1]

The expression "Sabba" (صبّ), which originally means pouring down water, here indicates the intensity and continuity of this punishment. It may also refer to cleansing the face of the earth from the existence of these tyrants. In any case, the most suitable meaning of "Sawt" is the first one, "whip"—an expression also common in everyday speech when we say someone struck the whip of punishment upon the enemy's back.

This brief expression refers to the severe and varied punishments that befell these nations. "‘Ad," according to the Holy Quran, was destroyed by a cold and piercing wind: "And as for 'Ad, they were destroyed by a screaming, violent wind" (Al-Haqqah: 6).

As for the people of Thamud, they were destroyed by a great heavenly blast: "So as for Thamud, they were destroyed by the overpowering [blast]" (Al-Haqqah: 5).

And as for the people of Pharaoh, they were drowned and buried amidst the waves of the Nile: "So We drowned them all" (Az-Zukhruf: 55).

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 16.

In the final verse under discussion, as a warning to all those who walk in the path of those rebellious nations, He says: "Indeed, your Lord is in ambush" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ). "Mirsad" (مرصاد) is from the root "Rasad" (رصد), meaning readiness to watch over something, and its English equivalent is "ambush" or "watchtower." This word is usually used in a place where individuals are forced to pass through a passage, and someone is ready in that passage to strike them. Overall, it indicates: do not think anyone can escape the clutches of divine punishment; all are within the grip of His power, and whenever He wills, He punishes them.

Obviously, Allah does not occupy a physical space and does not sit in a passage. This expression is a metaphor for the Lord's power encompassing all tyrants, rebels, and criminals. Therefore, it is narrated in a Hadith from Ali عليه السلام that the meaning of this verse is:

"Indeed, your Lord is capable of giving the people of disobedience their recompense." [1]

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said:

"The Mirsad is a bridge on the Sirat; no servant who owes a debt for an act of oppression against another servant shall pass it." [2]

This is, in fact, the mention of one clear instance, for the divine ambush is not limited to the Resurrection and the famous Bridge of Sirat. Allah is in ambush for the oppressor in this world as well, and the punishment of the three previous nations is a prominent example of it.

The expression "Your Lord" (ربّک) indicates that the divine Sunnah (law) regarding rebellious and oppressive nations will also apply to your Ummah. It is both a consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers—so they know that these stubborn, spiteful enemies will never escape the grip of God's power—and a warning to those who inflicted every kind of oppression upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers. They must know that those who were more powerful and stronger than them could not withstand a gale, a storm, or a single spark and heavenly blast; how do they think they can escape divine punishment with their wrongful deeds?

In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "The Trustworthy Spirit (Gabriel) informed me that when the One God halts the creation from the first to the last on the scene of Resurrection, He brings Hell and places the Sirat—which is thinner than a hair and sharper than a sword—over it. Upon the Sirat are three bridges: on the first bridge are trust, integrity, mercy, and love! On the second bridge is prayer! And on the third bridge is the justice of the Lord of the worlds! People are commanded to pass over it; those who fell short in trust and mercy will be stopped at the first bridge, and if they pass it, if they fell short in prayer, they will be stopped at the second bridge, and if they pass that, at the end of the path they will stand before Divine Justice, and this is the meaning of the verse: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ." [1]

In the words of Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"If Allah gives respite to the oppressor, His punishment will never escape him; He is in ambush for him on his path, and at the place where the bone sticks in the throat [making it impossible to swallow]." [2]

[1] and [2]: Majma‘ al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 487. [1]: Rawdat al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 573 (adapted). [2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 97.

Ayahs

فَأَمَّا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَأَكْرَمَهُۥ وَنَعَّمَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَكْرَمَنِ (١٥)
As for man, whenever his Lord tests him, and grants him honour, and blesses him, he says, ‘My Lord has honoured me.’ (15)
وَأَمَّآ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَهَـٰنَنِ (١٦)
But when He tests him and tightens for him his provision, he says, ‘My Lord has humiliated me.’ (16)
كَلَّا ۖ بَل لَّا تُكْرِمُونَ ٱلْيَتِيمَ (١٧)
No indeed! No, you do not honour the orphan, (17)
وَلَا تَحَـٰٓضُّونَ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (١٨)
and do not urge the feeding of the needy. (18)
وَتَأْكُلُونَ ٱلتُّرَاثَ أَكْلًا لَّمًّا (١٩)
You eat the inheritance rapaciously, (19)
وَتُحِبُّونَ ٱلْمَالَ حُبًّا جَمًّا (٢٠)
and love wealth with much fondness. (20)

Commentary

Neither be Deluded by His Blessing, Nor Despair at Its Withdrawal!

Following the previous verses that warned the transgressors and threatened them with divine punishment, the verses under discussion address the issue of "testing" (trial), which is the criterion for divine reward and punishment and is considered the most important matter in human life.

First, It states: "But as for man, when his Lord tries him and [thus] is generous to him and favors him, he [becomes deluded and] says, 'My Lord has honored me!'" (فَأَمَّا الْإِنْسانُ إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ رَبُّهُ فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَ نَعَّمَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ‌).

He does not realize that divine testing is sometimes through blessings and sometimes through various afflictions; neither should the arrival of a blessing be a cause for pride, nor should afflictions be a cause for despair and hopelessness. However, this short-sighted human forgets the purpose of the trial in both situations. When a blessing arrives, he imagines that he has become close to Allah's court, and that this blessing is proof of that proximity.

It is noteworthy that at the beginning of the verse, it says Allah "honors" him (bestows favor), but at the end of the verse, the human is blamed for seeing himself as "honored" by Allah. This is because the first "honor" (ikram) is in the sense of "bestowing favor" (in'am), while the second "honor" refers to "status and proximity" in the court of Allah.

"But when He tries him and restricts his provision, he [becomes despaired and] says, 'My Lord has humiliated me!'" (وَ أَمَّا إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَهانَنِ‌).

Despair envelops his entire being, and he becomes resentful and dissatisfied with his Lord, oblivious to the fact that these are all means of his trial and testing—a test that is the secret to human growth and perfection, and subsequently the cause for deserving reward, or in the case of opposition, the cause for deserving punishment.

These two verses warn that neither the arrival of a blessing is proof of proximity to Allah, nor is the withdrawal of a blessing proof of distance from the Truth. These are different "test materials" with which Allah, according to His wisdom, tests every group. It is short-sighted humans who are sometimes deluded and sometimes despaired.

In verse 51 of Surah Fussilat, it is also stated: وَ إِذا أَنْعَمْنا عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ أَعْرَضَ وَ نَأى‌ بِجانِبِهِ وَ إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعاءٍ عَرِيضٍ‌: "And when We bestow favor upon man, he turns away and distances himself; but when evil touches him, he is full of lengthy supplication."

And in verse 9 of Surah Hud, it says: وَ لَئِنْ أَذَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ مِنَّا رَحْمَةً ثُمَّ نَزَعْناها مِنْهُ إِنَّهُ لَيَؤُسٌ كَفُورٌ: "And if We give man a taste of mercy from Us and then We withdraw it from him, indeed, he is despairing and ungrateful."

In addition to pointing out the issue of divine testing through various means, these two verses also yield the conclusion that we must never consider the enjoyment of blessings or the deprivation of them as proof of one's personality and status before Allah, or distance from His holy presence. Rather, the criterion always and everywhere is faith and piety (Taqwa).

How many prophets were afflicted with various calamities in this world, and conversely, how many oppressive disbelievers enjoyed various blessings? This is the nature of worldly life.

Furthermore, this verse also contains a subtle allusion to the philosophy of afflictions and painful events.

Then, It proceeds to describe the actions that cause distance from Allah and entrapment in the clutches of divine punishment, stating: "No! [It is not as you imagine, that your wealth is proof of your status before your Lord; rather, your actions indicate your distance from Allah.] But you do not honor the orphan" (كَلَّا بَلْ لا تُكْرِمُونَ الْيَتِيمَ‌).

"And you do not encourage one another to feed the poor" (وَ لا تَحَاضُّونَ عَلى‌ طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ‌).

It is noteworthy that regarding orphans, It does not speak of "feeding," but rather of "honoring." This is because, in the case of an orphan, the issue is not merely hunger; more importantly, it is the compensation for his emotional deficiencies. An orphan should not feel that because he has lost his father, he has become humiliated, wretched, and worthless. He must be honored in such a way that he does not feel the void left by his father. Therefore, in Islamic narrations, special importance is given to the issue of showing affection and kindness to orphans.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "No servant strokes the head of an orphan out of mercy for him, except that Allah grants him a light for every hair [under his hand] on the Day of Resurrection." ^1 In verse 9 of Surah ad-Duha, it is also stated: فَأَمَّا الْيَتِيمَ فَلا تَقْهَرْ: "So as for the orphan, do not oppress [or humiliate] him."

This is exactly the opposite of what was and is prevalent in societies distant from faith and morality, such as the society of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) of yesterday and today; where they not only resort to various tricks to seize the property of orphans but also leave the orphan so alone in society that he feels the pain of orphanhood and the loss of a father in the most bitter way.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that "honoring orphans" is not limited to protecting their property, as some commentators have imagined, but has a broad and extensive meaning that includes both that and other matters.

The word "tahaddun" is derived from the root "hadd," meaning to urge and encourage. This indicates that merely feeding the poor is not enough; rather, people must encourage one another toward this good deed so that this tradition spreads throughout the atmosphere of society. [^2] Surprisingly, in verse 34 of Surah al-Haqqah, this subject is mentioned alongside the lack of faith in Allah the Great, stating: إِنَّهُ كانَ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ الْعَظِيمِ وَ لا يَحُضُّ عَلى‌ طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ‌: "Indeed, he did not used to believe in Allah, the Most Great, nor did he encourage the feeding of the poor." [^3]

^1: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 15, p. 120 (old edition). [^2]: "Tahaddun" was originally "tatahaddun," from which one "ta" was removed for ease of pronunciation. [^3]: "Ta'am" in this verse and the verse under discussion has an infinitive meaning, signifying "feeding" (it'am).

Then, It points to their third ugly deed and rebukes them, adding: "And you consume inheritance, devouring it altogether [whether lawful or unlawful]" (وَ تَأْكُلُونَ التُّراثَ أَكْلًا لَمًّا) ^1. Undoubtedly, consuming property that has reached a person through legitimate inheritance is not a blameworthy act. Therefore, the rebuke of this act in the above verse may refer to one of the following matters:

First, it refers to combining one's own right with the rights of others, as the word "lamm" originally means "gathering" or "collecting." Some commentators, like Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, have specifically interpreted it as gathering "lawful" and "unlawful" together. This is especially relevant because the custom of the pre-Islamic Arabs was to deprive women and children of inheritance and take their rights for themselves, believing that inheritance should only go to those who are warriors (since much of their wealth was obtained through raiding, they considered only those capable of raiding to be entitled to a share!).

Second, it means that when an inheritance reaches you, you do not spend any of it on poor relatives and the deprived of society. When you act this way with inherited wealth that is obtained without effort, you will certainly be even more stingy and restrictive regarding the income from your own labor, and this is a great flaw.

Third, it refers to consuming the inheritance of orphans and the rights of minors. It has often been seen that faithless or reckless individuals, when they get their hands on inherited property, show no consideration for the orphan or the minor. They take maximum advantage of the fact that these children are unable to defend their rights, and this is among the ugliest and most shameful of sins.

Combining all three interpretations is also possible. ^1

Then, It addresses their fourth reprehensible act, adding: "And you love wealth with immense love" (وَ تُحِبُّونَ الْمالَ حُبًّا جَمًّا) [^2]. You are worldly people, hoarders of wealth, enamored and infatuated with the goods and possessions of this world. Certainly, someone who has such an extraordinary attachment to wealth and property does not consider the lawful or unlawful, or the legitimate or illegitimate, when collecting it. Furthermore, such a person does not pay the divine rights (dues) upon it at all, or pays them insufficiently. Also, for such a person whose heart is entirely occupied by the love of wealth, there is no room for the remembrance of Allah in his heart.

In this way, after mentioning the testing of humans through blessings and afflictions, It draws their attention to four important tests in which this criminal group had failed and been rejected: 1. The test regarding orphans. 2. Feeding the poor. 3. The test of collecting shares of inheritance through lawful and unlawful means. 4. And finally, the test of accumulating wealth without any restrictions.

^1: "Lamm" means collecting, and sometimes it is used to mean collecting combined with fixing or settling.

[^2]: "Jamm," as stated in Misbah al-Lughah and Maqayis, means "much" and "abundant." "Jummah" (on the scale of jubbah) refers to the hair gathered at the front of the head.

And it is remarkable that all these tests are of a financial nature. In reality, if someone passes financial tests, other tests will be easier for him.

It is this worldly wealth and property that, as the saying goes, "has cast the faith of the heavens to the wind." Why are the greatest slips of the children of Adam in this very area?

There are those who are trustworthy up to a certain "amount" of wealth, but when their cup is full and it exceeds that limit, the whispers of Satan lead them to betrayal. True believers are those who observe trustworthiness, integrity, and the mandatory and recommended rights of others regarding any amount of wealth and without any conditions. Such people can claim faith and piety.

In short, those who pass financial tests of any size and quantity and under any circumstances are trustworthy, pious, God-fearing, and honorable individuals, and are considered the best of friends and companions. They are (usually) pure and upright individuals in other areas as well. The emphasis of the above verses on financial tests is for this very reason.

Ayahs

كَلَّآ إِذَا دُكَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا (٢١)
No indeed! When the earth is levelled to a plain, (21)
وَجَآءَ رَبُّكَ وَٱلْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا (٢٢)
and your Lord [’s edict] arrives with the angels in ranks, (22)
وَجِا۟ىٓءَ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِجَهَنَّمَ ۚ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ (٢٣)
the day when hell is brought [near], man will take admonition on that day, but what will that admonition avail him? (23)
يَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى قَدَّمْتُ لِحَيَاتِى (٢٤)
He will say, ‘Alas, had I sent ahead for my life [in the Hereafter]!’ (24)
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُعَذِّبُ عَذَابَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ (٢٥)
On that day none shall punish as He punishes, (25)
وَلَا يُوثِقُ وَثَاقَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ (٢٦)
and none shall bind as He binds. (26)

Commentary

They Wake Up on a Day When It Is Too Late!

Following the rebukes in the preceding verses against the worldly-minded transgressors and those who violate the rights of others, these verses warn them that there is ultimately a Resurrection, and an accounting and severe punishment lie ahead; they must prepare themselves for it.

First, He states: It is not as they imagine (that there is no accounting, and that if Allah gave them wealth and property, it was due to their status rather than as a trial and test) (كَلَّا - "No!").

"When the earth is pounded to powder, waste and scattered" (إِذا دُكَّتِ الْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا).

The word dakk (دك) originally means soft and level ground, and subsequently, it has been applied to the crushing of heights and buildings and leveling them. Dukkan (shop/stall) refers to a place that is level and without unevenness, and dakkeh refers to a platform that has been leveled and prepared for sitting.

The repetition of dakk in the above verse is for emphasis.

Altogether, this expression refers to the earthquakes and world-shaking events at the end of this world and the beginning of the Resurrection. Such a tremor will occur in the foundations of existence that the mountains will all be shattered and the lands will become level and smooth, as stated in Surah Ta-Ha, verses 106-108: وَيَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْجِبَالِ فَقُلْ يَنْسِفُهَا رَبِّي نَسْفًا فَيَذَرُهَا قَاعًا صَفْصَفًا لَا تَرَىٰ فِيهَا عِوَجًا وَلَا أَمْتًا: "And they ask you about the mountains, so say: My Lord will blow them away with a blast. Then He will leave it as a level, smooth plain. You will see therein no crookedness or any unevenness."

After the completion of the first stage of Resurrection—namely, the destruction of the world—the second stage begins, and all humans return to life and appear in the court of Divine Justice: "And your Lord comes, and the angels, rank upon rank" (وَ جاءَ رَبُّكَ وَ الْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا). They surround those gathered in the assembly and stand ready to execute the Command of the Truth.

This is a depiction of the grandeur of that Great Day and man's inability to escape the clutches of justice.

The expression "Your Lord comes" (ja'a rabbuka) is a metaphor for the arrival of Allah's Command to settle the accounts of the creation.

Alternatively, it refers to the manifestation of the signs of His greatness and the tokens of Allah.

Or, the "appearance of the Lord" refers to the manifestation of the knowledge of Him on that day in such a way that no room remains for anyone to deny Him; it is as if everyone observes His Peerless Essence with their own eyes. In any case, it is certain that the "coming" of Allah in the literal sense—which would necessitate Him being a physical body and moving through space—is meaningless, for He is far removed from being a body or having physical properties [1]. This same meaning has been explicitly narrated in a tradition from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام.

[1]: Fakhr al-Razi says in his commentary: The verse has an omitted word (ellipsis), and this omitted word might be "Command" (amr), "Power" (qahr), "Great Signs" (jala'il al-ayat), or "Manifestation and Knowledge." Other commentators have also mentioned some of these four terms, especially the first one, as the implied meaning of the verse.

The evidence for this interpretation is verse 33 of Surah An-Nahl, which states: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ: "Do they wait for anything except that the angels should come to them or that the Command of your Lord should come?" The expression "rank upon rank" (saffan saffa) indicates that the angels enter the gathering in various rows, and possibly the angels of each heaven appear in one rank and surround the people of the assembly.

Then He adds: "And Hell is brought near that Day; on that Day man will remember, but how will that remembrance profit him?" (وَ جِي‌ءَ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِجَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ وَ أَنَّى لَهُ الذِّكْرى‌).

From this expression, it is understood that Hell is capable of being moved, and it is brought close to the criminals! Just as we read regarding Paradise in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 90: وَ أُزْلِفَتِ الْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ: "And Paradise will be brought near to the righteous." Although some prefer to interpret these as metaphorical, signifying the appearance of Paradise and Hell before the eyes of the virtuous and the wicked, there is no reason to deviate from the literal meaning. Rather, it is better to leave it as it appears, for the realities of the world of Resurrection are not precisely clear to us, and the conditions governing that place differ greatly from here; there is no obstacle to Paradise and Hell changing locations on that day.

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 416.

In a tradition from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that when the above verse (وَ جِي‌ءَ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِجَهَنَّمَ) was revealed, the color of his blessed face changed. This state was distressing for his companions. Some went to Ali عليه السلام and explained the situation. Ali عليه السلام came, kissed the area between the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله shoulders, and said: "O Messenger of Allah! May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, what has happened?"

He replied: "Gabriel came and recited this verse to me."

Ali عليه السلام says: I asked: "How will they bring Hell?"

He replied: "Seventy thousand angels will pull it with seventy thousand reins! And it will be in such a state of rebellion that if they were to release it, it would burn everyone. Then I will stand before Hell, and it will say: 'O Muhammad! I have no business with you; Allah has forbidden your flesh upon me.' On that day, everyone will be thinking of themselves, but Muhammad will say: 'My Lord, my Ummah! My Ummah!'" [1]

Indeed, when the criminal human sees these scenes, he is shaken and wakes up; a halo of grief and sorrow envelops him. He looks back at his past and deeply regrets his actions, but this regret is of no use.

Man wishes to return and compensate for the dark past, but the doors of return are completely closed.

He wants to repent, but the time for repentance has passed.

He wants to perform righteous deeds to make up for his evil deeds, but the record of deeds has been folded up.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 483. A similar meaning is also mentioned in the commentary Durr al-Manthur (as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 415).

It is here that his cry rises: "He will say: 'Oh, I wish I had sent forth [good deeds] for my life!'" (يَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي قَدَّمْتُ لِحَياتِي).

It is interesting to note that he does not say "for my afterlife," but rather "for my life," as if the word "life" (hayat) is not worthy of anything other than the life of the hereafter, and the fleeting life of this world, mixed with all sorts of calamities, is not considered true life.

As we read in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 64: وَ ما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَ لَعِبٌ وَ إِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ: "And this worldly life is nothing but diversion and amusement, and indeed, the home of the Hereafter—that is [true] life, if only they knew."

Yes, those who plundered the property of orphans, did not put a morsel in the mouths of the hungry, snatched inheritance from this person and that, and whose hearts were entirely conquered by the love of worldly wealth, will wish on that day that they had sent something forward for the life of the hereafter, which is the true and eternal life. But this is a futile wish that will never lead anywhere.

Then, in two short sentences, He describes the intensity of Divine punishment on that day, saying: "So on that Day, none will punish as He punishes" (فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لا يُعَذِّبُ عَذابَهُ أَحَدٌ).

Indeed, these transgressors who, in their time of power, committed the worst crimes and sins, will be punished on that day in a manner that has no precedent, just as the virtuous will see such rewards that have not even crossed anyone's mind. For He is, in one respect, the "Most Merciful of the merciful," and in another, the "Severest of punishers."

"And none will bind as He binds" (وَ لا يُوثِقُ وَثاقَهُ أَحَدٌ).

Neither His bonds and chains have an equal, nor His punishment and torment. Why should it not be so, when they also, in this world, bound the oppressed servants of Allah as much as they had the power to do so, and subjected them to the most severe tortures?

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيَّتُهَا ٱلنَّفْسُ ٱلْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ (٢٧)
‘O soul at peace! (27)
ٱرْجِعِىٓ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ رَاضِيَةً مَّرْضِيَّةً (٢٨)
Return to your Lord, pleased and pleasing! (28)
فَٱدْخُلِى فِى عِبَـٰدِى (٢٩)
Then enter among My servants, (29)
وَٱدْخُلِى جَنَّتِى (٣٠)
and enter My paradise!’ (30)

Commentary

O Possessor of the Reassured Soul!

After mentioning the horrific punishment that will seize the transgressors and the world-worshippers on the Day of Resurrection, the verses under discussion turn to the opposite point. They address the "reassured souls" (Nufus Mutma'innah) and the believers who enjoy complete tranquility amidst that great storm, addressing them with a world of grace and affection, saying: "O reassured soul!" (يَا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ).

"Return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing [to Him]"! (ارْجِعِي إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ رَاضِيَةً مَرْضِيَّةً).

"And enter among My [righteous] servants" (فَادْخُلِي فِي عِبَادِي).

"And enter My Paradise" (وَادْخُلِي جَنَّتِي).

What fascinating, delightful, and soul-nourishing expressions! Grace, purity, tranquility, and assurance pour from them. A direct invitation from the Lord to the souls who, in the light of faith, have reached a state of assurance and peace.

An invitation for them to return to their Lord—to their Owner, Nourisher, and Reformer.

An invitation blended with mutual satisfaction: the satisfaction of the devoted lover with the Beloved, and the satisfaction of the true Beloved and Deity.

Following that, placing the crown of the honor of servitude upon their head, honoring them with the garment of bondage, and placing them in the ranks of His chosen ones! Then, inviting them to enter Paradise, and that too with the expression "Enter My Paradise," which shows that the Host of this banquet is none other than His Sacred Essence. What an invitation! What a Host! And what a guest!

The word "Nafs" (soul) here refers to the human spirit.

The description "Mutma'innah" (reassured/tranquil) refers to the peace that is found and achieved in the light of faith, as the Quran says: "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured" (أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ) (Ar-Ra'd: 28).

Such a soul is assured of the divine promises, certain of the path and method it has chosen, steady both in the prosperity of the world and its adversity, in storms, and in accidents and calamities. Above all, it is calm during that great horror, terror, and anxiety of the Day of Resurrection.

According to a group of commentators, the meaning of "returning to your Lord" is returning to His reward and mercy. However, it is better to say it means returning to Him Himself—that is, taking a place in the proximity of His presence; a spiritual and internal return, not a physical or spatial one.

Is this invitation to return to the Lord only on the Day of Resurrection, or from the moment of death and the end of life?

The context of the verses certainly relates to the Resurrection, although the expression of this verse itself is absolute and broad.

The term "Radiyah" (well-pleased) is used because the soul sees all the divine promises of reward as more real than it had imagined, and such grace and mercy from Allah encompass it that it becomes entirely filled with satisfaction and pleasure. As for the term "Mardiyyah" (pleasing), it is because the soul has become accepted and pleasing to the Friend (Allah).

Such a servant, with such attributes, and having reached the station of complete satisfaction and submission, has realized the reality of servitude—which is giving up everything in the way of the Deity—and has stepped into the ranks of Allah's special servants. Certainly, there is no place for such a person but Paradise.

In some commentaries, it is mentioned that these verses were revealed regarding "Hamzah, the Prince of Martyrs," but considering that this Surah is Meccan, this is actually a type of application (Tatbiq) rather than the specific occasion of revelation (Sha'n al-Nuzul), just as we read regarding Imam Hussain (عليه السلام) at the beginning of the Surah.

Interestingly, in a narration quoted in Al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: One of his companions asked: "Is it possible for a believer to be dissatisfied with the taking of his soul?" He (عليه السلام) replied: "No, by Allah! When the Angel of Death comes to take his soul, the believer shows distress. The Angel of Death says: 'O friend of Allah, do not be distressed! By Him who sent Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله), I am more kind to you than a compassionate father. Open your eyes and look.' He looks and sees the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), Fatimah (عليها السلام), Hasan (عليه السلام), Hussain (عليه السلام), and the Imams from his progeny (عليهم السلام). The angel says to him: 'Look, these are the Messenger of Allah, the Commander of the Faithful, Fatimah, Hasan, Hussain, and the Imams (عليهم السلام); they are your friends.'

He opens his eyes and looks; suddenly a caller from the side of the Great Lord calls out and says: 'O you who had assurance in Muhammad and his family! Return to your Lord, while you are pleased with their Wilayah (guardianship), and He is pleased with you through His reward. Enter among My servants—meaning Muhammad and his Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام)—and enter My Paradise.' At that moment, nothing is more beloved to the person than for his soul to separate from his body as quickly as possible and join this caller!" [1]

O Allah! Honor us with such tranquility that we may become worthy of this great address.

[1]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 3, Chapter: "The Believer is not forced upon the taking of his soul," Hadith 2.

O Lord! Reaching this station is not possible except by Your grace and favor; include us in Your favors.

O Allah! Certainly, nothing will be diminished from Your generosity if You place us among the possessors of reassured souls; come and grant us this favor and show generosity.

O Lord! We know that this tranquility is not possible except under the shadow of Your remembrance; grant us the success of remembrance Yourself.

Amin, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Fajr and end of Volume 26 of Tafsir Nemouneh. 7th of Ramadan al-Mubarak, 1407 AH.
